New York Independent System Operator · Reliability Research Prototype

Can we count power outages — and model them?

A plain-language tour of the data behind a New York electric-grid reliability study: what each dataset is, what the exploratory analysis shows, and why the headline model is a Negative Binomial we cannot yet run.

months (Nov 2022–Jan 2024)
11
NYISO load zones
10

114,323

100%

Contents
  1. 1The big picture
  2. 2Words you'll need

Section 1

The big picture, for someone who has never seen this before

NYISO — the New York Independent System Operator — is the organization that keeps the lights on across New York State. Every second it balances how much electricity is being produced against how much is being used, and it runs the wholesale marketplace where electricity is bought and sold. To do this it publishes a steady stream of public data: how much power each region used, what it cost, and which pieces of equipment were out of service.

An outage is simply a generator or a transmission line being out of service — sometimes planned (maintenance), sometimes forced (a fault or a storm). Outages are the reliability story: too many at the wrong time is how you get shortages and price spikes.

The research question

Main track: On a given day, in a given region, how many new outage events happen — and is that count so erratic that it needs a special statistical model (a Negative Binomial) rather than a simple one?
Extension track: Use demand, weather and prices to describe price stress (the gap between planned and real-time electricity prices).

The honest headline, which the rest of this page builds toward: we successfully collected and cleaned the demand, price and weather data, but none of the outage data we have can be turned into a trustworthy count of events. So the descriptive analysis is real and done; the count model is designed but deliberately not yet run.

Section 2

A few words you'll need

Five ideas make everything below readable. None is complicated.

Zone

NYISO splits the state into 11 pricing regions (New York City, Long Island, Capital/Albany, West, …). Demand, price and outages are all reported per zone, and the zones differ enormously in size.

Load

The amount of electricity being used, in megawatts (MW). "Actual load" is what really happened; "load forecast" is what NYISO predicted in advance.

LBMP

The price of electricity at a specific location, in $/MWh. There are two markets: Day-Ahead (locked in a day early) and Real-Time (set as it actually happens).

DART

DART = Real-Time − Day-Ahead price. It measures surprise: when real-time prices jump above what was planned, the grid was under stress. We treat it as a descriptor, never as proof of a cause.

Time & daylight saving

Every timestamp is in New York local time. Twice a year the clock jumps, and one autumn hour happens twice — a real trap the cleaning step has to handle without guessing.

Section 3

The datasets, one by one

Ten public feeds were downloaded, in four families. Each card says what it is in plain terms, what we use it for, and the single most important thing to remember about it. The codes like P-58B are just NYISO's names for each report.

Electricity demand — how much power is used

Actual load

P-58B

How many megawatts each zone actually used, measured every 5 minutes.

Used for: the demand backbone of every analysis; peaks and daily rhythm.

● Takeaway. Clean and complete — 1.48M rows across 15 months.

Load forecast

P-7

What NYISO predicted each zone would use, published ahead of time, hourly.

Used for: forecast error (actual − predicted), a price-stress input.

● Watch. You must use the right forecast "vintage" — never mix in hindsight.
Electricity prices — what it cost

Day-Ahead price

P-2A

The price locked in one day early, per zone, per hour. The market's plan.

Used for: the "DA" half of DART.

● Takeaway. Smooth and well-behaved; averages about $37/MWh.

Real-Time price

P-24A

The price as it actually cleared, every 5 minutes. The market's reality.

Used for: the "RT" half of DART; a stress signal.

● Watch. Spiky — ranges from −$900 to +$3,200/MWh. It can be a consequence of outages, not a cause.
Outages — equipment out of service (the hard part)

Generation outage forecast

P-15

A single forecast of how many megawatts of generation will be offline over the coming weeks.

Used for: illustrating total outage size — not events.

● Limit. It is MW, not a count, and the snapshot is dated 2026 — it doesn't overlap our study window.

Transmission outages

P-54A/B/C

Line-by-line records of transmission equipment out of service — the closest thing to real outage records we have. A photo of the whole system taken every few minutes.

Used for: (future) transmission-outage detail.

● Limit. Repeated snapshots with no stable event ID: one outage appears in thousands of rows, so you cannot count events. ~20M rows each.

Outage schedule

P-14B

A list of outages with a unique Outage ID and start/end times.

Used for: understanding what a clean outage record looks like.

● Limit. Only today's snapshot is public — no history, so you can't rebuild what happened each day in 2022–2024.
Weather — the driver behind demand

Historical weather

Open-Meteo

Hourly temperature, precipitation, wind and humidity for Albany.

Used for: explaining demand and validating the weather pipeline.

● Watch. Only one location — it can't represent every zone's weather. Downloaded in UTC to dodge the daylight-saving trap.
Section 4

How the raw files become analysis-ready

Downloaded files are never used directly. They flow through three layers, so every step is reproducible and the original files are never overwritten.

raw

As downloaded

Exact NYISO archives and weather files, untouched.

→
interim

Cleaned

One tidy table per source: consistent columns, correct time zone.

→
processed

Panels

Everything aligned into a daily panel and an hourly price panel.

→
outputs

Figures & report

The charts and tables in this document.

The daylight-saving story — a quiet mark of quality

On 5 November 2023 the clock fell back, so 1:00–1:59 a.m. happened twice. A naive timestamp like "01:30" then has two possible meanings. The cleaning code refuses to guess: for actual load it reads the file's own EDT/EST label; for prices it uses the order rows were written; and weather is fetched in universal time so the ambiguity never arises. Where nothing can resolve it, the code raises an error rather than inventing a reading. That is the difference between data that looks clean and data that is clean.

Section 5

What the data shows — and what it means

Seven charts from the cleaned demand, weather and price data. Each one comes with a reading: not just what the line does, but why it matters for a reliability study.

Demand · seasonality

Electricity demand is driven by the seasons

Summed across all 11 zones, demand climbs to a sharp summer peak (July and early September 2023, near 24,000 MW) as air-conditioning loads the grid, with a gentler winter rise for heating and long troughs in the mild spring and fall.

Why it matters: reliability risk is not evenly spread. An outage during a July heat peak is far more dangerous than the same outage in mild May. Any model of outages or prices has to control for season — it is the strongest rhythm in the whole dataset.

Demand · daily rhythm

And by the hour of the day

Averaged over every day, load bottoms out around 4–5 a.m., ramps up through the morning, and peaks in the evening as people come home, cook, and switch on lights and cooling.

Why it matters: the evening is when the grid works hardest and has the least spare margin. Anything analysed hour-by-hour — prices, price stress — must account for this shape, or the evening will masquerade as a "finding".

Demand × weather

Both hot and cold push demand up

Plotting demand against temperature reveals the classic U-shape: consumption is lowest in the mild middle (around 10–15 °C) and rises at both ends — heating when it is cold, cooling when it is hot. The separate horizontal bands are simply zones of very different size.

Why it matters: the demand–weather link is real but non-linear, so temperature can't enter a model as a single straight line. It also exposes a limitation: one Albany weather station can't capture what every zone actually felt.

Structure · zones

The zones are not remotely alike

Average demand ranges from the very large downstate and big upstate zones down to small ones a fraction of their size. A "typical" zone does not exist.

Why it matters: you cannot pool zones into one number — a model needs a per-zone structure (a zone effect). This is exactly why the outage target would have to be defined per zone per day, not as a single statewide total.

Prices · plan vs reality

The plan is smooth; reality is jumpy

The day-ahead price (planned a day early) tracks calmly, while the real-time price spikes around it. On average they agree closely, but real-time carries sudden excursions the plan didn't see coming.

Why it matters: the gap between the two — where the market was surprised — is precisely where reliability events tend to live. That gap is the next chart.

Price stress · DART

Usually calm, occasionally violent

DART (real-time minus day-ahead) piles up tightly around zero — most hours the plan was right — but has heavy tails: 3.5% of hours land beyond ±$50, with extremes past ±$3,000. The average is a mere $0.41, yet the standard deviation is $60.

Why it matters: the story is in the rare extremes, not the average. This "small mean, fat tails" shape is the same kind of behaviour — a few big, clustered events — that makes a Negative Binomial the right tool for outage counts (Section 6).

Price stress · timing

Stress clusters at the busy hours

The average DART stays near zero all day, but its volatility (the spread) swells through the afternoon and evening — the same hours demand peaks.

Why it matters: price surprises aren't random through the day; they concentrate when the system is already stretched. Timing is a genuine signal, and confirms price stress should never be read without the hour-of-day in hand.

Outages · a cautionary chart

Why P-15 can't be the outcome we model

This is the one outage chart we can draw: forecasted generation megawatts offline. Notice two things — the vertical axis is MW, not a number of events, and the dates are in 2026, outside our 2022–2024 window.

Why it matters: it visually makes the core point. "How much capacity is out" is not "how many outages happened." Modeling a count needs the latter — which none of our outage feeds provide.

Section 6

The headline model: a Negative Binomial — and why it's the right choice

The main research question is about a count: how many new outage events occur in a zone on a day. Counts are whole numbers that can't go below zero (0, 1, 2, 3…), so ordinary regression — which happily predicts 2.7 or −1 — is the wrong instrument. There is a family of models built for counts.

Y[zone, day] = number of new outage events  →  a non-negative integer, e.g.  CAPITL, 2023-01-01 → 3

Start: the Poisson model

The textbook count model. Its one big assumption is that the average equals the variance — events arrive at a steady, even rate.

That holds for truly random, independent events. Outages are not like that.

Fix: the Negative Binomial

Outages cluster — a heatwave or storm triggers many at once — so the variance runs far larger than the mean. This is called overdispersion.

The Negative Binomial adds one extra "dispersion" dial that lets the spread exceed the average, so it fits clumpy, bursty counts honestly.

The plan, then, is disciplined and simple: build the count, check whether its variance really does exceed its mean (the variance-to-mean ratio), and if so prefer the Negative Binomial over Poisson — reporting both. The very same "few large, clustered events" pattern we already saw in the DART tails (Section 5) is what we expect the outage counts to show.

But first the outcome has to exist — and it doesn't yet

A model is only as good as its outcome variable. Before running anything, the target has to clear a short checklist. This is where the project honestly stops.

×
A stable event ID, so each outage is counted once
P-54 has none — the same outage repeats across thousands of snapshot rows.
×
A real start time for each event — when it newly occurred
Snapshots show a state, not a first-occurrence moment.
×
A whole-number count that is truly a count
P-15 is megawatts, not events — and it's dated 2026.
×
Full history across 2022–2024
P-14B has proper event IDs but only publishes today's snapshot — no past.
×
A facility→zone map and a forced/scheduled label
Not consistently available across the outage sources.
✓
Covariates ready: load, weather, price, forecast error
All cleaned and aligned — the right-hand side of the model is done.
Model status — blocked, not broken

Every needed predictor exists; the outcome does not. Because no outage feed yields a verified, historical, per-zone event count, the Negative Binomial is designed and gated but not run. This is a data-availability finding — a genuine result of source discovery — not a bug or a failure of the code.

Section 7

Where we stand

Done
  • 10 datasets downloaded, 15 months, 11 zones
  • Cleaned with explicit daylight-saving handling
  • Daily panel (4,763 rows) & hourly price panel (114,323 rows)
  • Day-ahead / real-time matched 100%
  • Descriptive EDA on demand, weather and prices
Blocked
  • No verified historical outage event count
  • P-54: repeated snapshots, no event ID
  • P-15: megawatts, wrong dates
  • P-14B: has IDs but no history
  • → Negative Binomial gated, not run
Next
  • Find event-level outage history with stable IDs
  • Process P-54 month-by-month (memory-safe)
  • Full DART price-stress descriptive analysis
  • Run Poisson vs Negative Binomial once a target exists
The one-sentence version

We built a clean, reproducible pipeline and a solid descriptive picture of New York's electricity demand, weather and prices — and discovered that the outage data available can't yet support the count model at the heart of the study, which is itself the most important thing to report.
